mnemonics.title: Graphics Pipeline Order
mnemonics.content: The order is: INPUT ASSEMBLER → VERTEX SHADER → TESSELLATION → GEOMETRY SHADER → RASTERIZER → FRAGMENT SHADER → OUTPUT MERGER. Remember: All Very Tall Giants Rarely Find Owls (AVTG-RFO).

mnemonics.title: Shader Types
mnemonics.content: Vertex transforms, Fragment colors (VFFC). Vertex for position, Fragment for final color - that's all you need to start!

mnemonics.title: Matrix Transformations Order
mnemonics.content: Model → View → Projection: Remember MVP = M(elting) V(anilla) P(opsicle). First apply Model (object space to world), then View (world to camera), then Projection (to screen).

acronyms.usage: The software interface through which programs interact with graphics hardware (OpenGL, DirectX, Vulkan).
GPU memory buffer storing vertex data (positions, normals, UVs).
Object encapsulating VBO bindings and vertex attribute configurations.

acronyms.expansion: Index Buffer Object (or Element Array Buffer)
acronyms.usage: Buffer storing indices referencing vertices in VBO for efficient primitive rendering.
Off-screen rendering target collection (color, depth, stencil buffers).
Efficient storage for sharing uniform data across shader programs.
High-level language for writing shaders in OpenGL/Vulkan.
Executes application code and issues draw calls to GPU.
Hardware optimized for parallel rendering operations.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an assembly line factory: Raw materials (vertices) enter → each station (shader stage) adds something → finished products (pixels) come out at the end.
Think of VBO as a list of unique workers and IBO as a schedule telling which workers to call for each team/project (triangle). Same workers can be reused without listing them again.
Think of a control panel with many switches (glEnable/glDisable). Each subsequent operation uses whatever switch settings are currently active until you change them.
Data flows like water through pipes: vertex shader injects variables at one end → they flow through the pipeline → fragment shader receives interpolated values at the other end.

analogies.concept: Graphics API as a Restaurant Menu
analogies.analogy: Just as a menu lists available dishes (functions) you can order without knowing the kitchen's internal operations, a graphics API provides functions to create visuals without needing hardware details.

A shader is like a recipe: it takes inputs (ingredients: vertices, textures, uniforms), follows instructions (shader code), and produces an output (colored pixels). Different recipes = different visual effects.
A render context is like a painter's canvas - it holds all current settings (brush type, colors, style) that will be applied to every stroke (draw call) until changed.
A framebuffer is like a photo frame holding multiple layers: the color layer (what you see), the depth layer (how far), and stencil layer (what to show). All combine to create the final image.
